g.data-narration: Welcome to C++ Access Control - learning how to grant proper access to class members
g.text: Granting Access in C++ Object-Oriented Programming

g.data-narration: Granting access in C++ controls which parts of your program can use private and protected class members
g.text: In OOP, granting access refers to mechanisms that control which external entities can access
private and protected members of a class while maintaining encapsulation.

g.data-narration: Encapsulation protects data integrity while controlled access provides necessary flexibility for complex programs
g.text: • Encapsulation protects internal data from unauthorized modification
• Controlled access provides flexibility while maintaining security
• Essential for inheritance, operator overloading, and cooperative class designs

g.data-narration: C++ provides three access specifiers: private most restrictive, protected allows derived class access, public accessible everywhere
Private members are only accessible within the declaring class and to friend functions, forming the core of encapsulation
g.text: • Accessible ONLY within the declaring class
• Default access specifier for class members

g.data-narration: Protected members add accessibility in derived classes beyond what private allows, essential for inheritance hierarchies
g.text: • Similar to private with ONE key difference

g.data-narration: Public members form the interface accessible from anywhere in the program, constituting the class's public API
g.text: • Accessible from ANYWHERE in the program
• Forms the class interface for external code

g.data-narration: The access hierarchy shows increasing accessibility: private is most restricted, protected adds derived class access, public opens everywhere
A friend function is a non-member function granted special access to private and protected members of a class
g.text: A friend function is a non-member function that has special permission
to access private and protected members of a class. Despite this access, it is NOT a member.
• Declared using the friend keyword inside the class

g.data-narration: Syntax uses friend keyword inside class, common for operator overloading. Friendship is not transitive or inherited
g.text: • Not a member function (no this pointer)
• Friendship is NOT transitive, inherited, or mutual

g.data-narration: A friend class gains access to all private and protected members of another class, useful for closely related classes
g.text: When class A declares class B as a friend, all member functions of B can access
the private and protected members of A. Useful when two classes share implementation details.

g.data-narration: To declare a friend class, use friend class ClassName; inside the granting class. All member functions become friends
g.text: Reader can now access Writer private members!
All member functions of Reader get access

g.data-narration: Static members also follow access control rules - they can be private, protected, or public like regular members
g.text: Static data members and static member functions also participate in C++ access control.
Same rules apply: private, protected, or public. They are shared among all objects of a class.

g.data-narration: Nested classes can access outer class private members, but the outer class cannot access nested private members unless granted friendship
g.text: C++ allows declaring classes within other classes. The nested class has access to the
outer class private members, but NOT vice versa unless friendship is explicitly granted.
Outer to Nested: Only if friend

g.data-narration: This complete example demonstrates access specifiers, friend function for operator overloading, and friend class usage
Key takeaways: Use private for encapsulation, protected for inheritance, public for interface, use friends sparingly to maintain encapsulation benefits
g.text: 1. Private: Class only + friends | Protected: Class + derived + friends | Public: Anywhere
2. Friend functions are NOT members but access private/protected members
3. Friendship is not transitive, inherited, or mutual - must be explicitly granted
4. Use friends judiciously - minimizes encapsulation benefits

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Remember access hierarchy, friend functions are non-members, friendship is not inherited, know correct syntax, and balance encapsulation with flexibility
g.text: Remember: Private less than Protected less than Public (accessibility hierarchy)
Friend functions are NOT member functions - no this pointer, use without dot operator
Friendship is NOT inherited or transitive - each class must explicitly grant
